hero.slogan: Discover the beauty hidden in Chinese patterns

explore.noCommunity: No community uploads yet. Be the first!

create.title: Make Your Own Pattern Art
create.fillHint: Tap a color below, then tap any region of the pattern to fill
create.doodleHint: Draw freely on the canvas — each stroke records a color

imperial.name: Imperial
imperial.desc: The most iconic and majestic palette of the Forbidden City — ideal for dragon, phoenix, and ruyi patterns.

0.name: Zhu Hong
0.desc: The classic palace wall red — high saturation, symbolising wealth and celebration.

1.name: Ming Huang
1.desc: The exclusive imperial colour — the brilliant gold of glazed tiles under sunlight.

2.name: Qiu Xiang
2.desc: A muted, noble yellow-green — highly favoured in Qing court garments.

3.name: Yu Se
3.desc: Lustrous jade green — symbolising the treasured heirloom jades of the Palace Museum.

4.name: Xuan Se
4.desc: The black of the cosmos — deep reddish-brown, the colour of palace pillars and bracket sets.

celadon.name: Celadon
celadon.desc: Drawn from Song dynasty Ru, Ge and blue-and-white porcelain — clear, elegant tones perfect for cloud and scroll patterns.

0.name: Tian Qing
0.desc: The classic Ru ware glaze — "After the rain, the sky clears and the clouds break — make this very colour."

1.name: Yue Bai
1.desc: The pale blue-white of moonlight — airy and luminous.

2.name: Shi Qing
2.desc: Traditional mineral pigment — blue-within-green, calm and profound.

3.name: Dai Lan
3.desc: "Distant mountains like indigo" — a classic deep blue with grey undertones.

4.name: Tu Bai
4.desc: Soft white like thatching grass — more antique and refined than pure white.

silk.name: Silk
silk.desc: Inspired by ornate court brocades and woven silks — soft, romantic, elegant, appealing to young audiences and creative design.

0.name: Hai Tian Xia
0.desc: Pale pink-orange refracted from sunset over the sea — a frequent colour in Ming-Qing official robes.

1.name: Tao Yao
1.desc: "The peach tree is young and elegant, its blossoms are splendid" — bright, delicate peach pink.

2.name: Yan Zhi
2.desc: The deep crimson of traditional rouge — rich, saturated, full of classical allure.

3.name: Ou He
3.desc: A gentle, elegant pale purple with a grey cast — exceptionally graceful.

4.name: Mu Shan Zi
4.desc: "Mist congeals and the mountains turn purple at dusk" — the mysterious pink-violet of twilight ridgelines.

botanical.name: Botanical
botanical.desc: Distilled from precious palace flora and flower-arranging — fresh, vibrant, quietly understated.

0.name: Pine Blossom
0.desc: A light, cheerful yellow-green — like the first pine blossom of spring.

1.name: Bamboo Green
1.desc: The lush, moist green of bamboo — calming and eye-soothing.

2.name: Gamboge
2.desc: Golden yellow — extracted from the gamboge tree resin, used in classical painting.

3.name: Tipsy Cheeks
3.desc: The flushed pink-orange of cheeks after a sip of wine — warm, gentle.

4.name: Mugwort Green
4.desc: The pale green of mugwort leaf undersides — hazy, elegant.

palette.title: Forbidden City Palettes
palette.description: Colours distilled from imperial architecture, court attire, official kiln porcelain & seasonal flora. Each palette is historically grounded — visually rich with Eastern classical beauty.

ink.name: Ink Wash
ink.desc: The five shades of Chinese ink — from jet black to pure white. The classical palette of calligraphy, landscape painting, and literati aesthetics.

0.name: Burnt Ink
0.desc: The deepest black — like freshly ground ink on the inkstone.

1.name: Dark Ink
1.desc: Rich, warm black — the second wash of the brush.

2.name: Grey Ink
2.desc: Mid-tone grey — the third wash, where form begins to soften.

3.name: Pale Ink
3.desc: Light silvery grey — distant mountains in a landscape scroll.

4.name: Paper White
4.desc: The white of xuan paper — the breath between brushstrokes.

dunhuang.name: Dunhuang
dunhuang.desc: Mineral pigments from the Mogao Caves — azurite, malachite, ochre, cinnabar, and gold. Ancient colours preserved on cave walls for over a thousand years.

0.name: Azurite
0.desc: Deep blue-green — ground from the mineral azurite, the sky in Buddhist murals.

1.name: Malachite
1.desc: Verdant green — from the mineral malachite, the hills and foliage of paradise.

2.name: Ochre
2.desc: Earthy golden-brown — iron oxide pigment, the warmth of desert sandstone.

3.name: Cinnabar
3.desc: Brilliant vermilion — mercury sulphide, the red of flying apsaras robes.

4.name: Gold Leaf
4.desc: Precious gold — applied as thin foil, the halo of Buddhas and bodhisattvas.

cloud.name: Cloud Pattern
cloud.story: The flowing curves of the cloud pattern symbolise good fortune drifting into one's life. In traditional Chinese art, clouds carry rain — the giver of harvest — and serve as the vehicle upon which immortals travel between heaven and earth.

ruyi.name: Ruyi Pattern
ruyi.story: The shape of Ruyi traces back to a back-scratcher that evolved into a scepter of authority and blessing. Its rounded, heart-like head represents "everything as you wish." For centuries, lovers exchanged Ruyi-shaped ornaments as tokens of devotion.

dragon.name: Dragon Pattern
dragon.story: Unlike the fire-breathing dragons of Western myth, the Chinese dragon is a guardian of water and sky — wise, benevolent, and a bringer of rain. Wrapped into a circular "tuan" form, it embodies wholeness, protection, and quiet power.

huiwen.name: Meander Pattern
huiwen.story: One of the oldest Chinese patterns, the meander — or "hui wen" — takes its name from the character 回 (return). Its continuous, unbroken line symbolises endlessness. You'll see it carved into bronze vessels from over 3,000 years ago.

ai.aiHint: AI palette added to the selector above
